narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like your upper-middle family income and choice to study full-time. The admissions model we use predicted a 50.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold that led to your application being rejected—it's designed to flag higher-risk profiles to ensure students are set up for success. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 1 school decile ranking of 3, well below the typical student's 5.71, signaling to the model that your early academic performance was lower than most who pass the bar. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 34, though under the average of 36.7, actually worked in your favor by pushing the prediction toward passing more than any other factor. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, below the usual 3.21, helped a bit by nudging things toward success, but not enough to offset the concerns. Your Year 3 decile of 3, again lower than the average 5.53, added some weight toward the failure prediction, while your family income and full-time status had minimal sway. Compared to typical students who pass, your grades and deciles stand out as notably lower, which overshadowed your solid LSAT and other positives in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar starts—but it explains the cautious decision here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score further, strengthening your early grades through additional coursework or explanations in a new application, and highlighting any recent improvements or unique experiences as a Black woman in law. Reapplying after addressing these key areas could shift the prediction in your favor, and we're rooting for your success. Keep pushing; your story matters.
